result:
# Static

The radio in the kitchen hasn't worked since March. My father keeps it on the counter anyway, between the coffee maker and the ceramic jar shaped like a rooster that holds the wooden spoons. Sometimes I think he forgets it's broken. Sometimes I think he knows perfectly well and leaves it there as a kind of monument to something he can't name.

I drove home last weekend. Four hours through flat country, past the grain elevators that appear on the horizon like sentences you've been meaning to finish. I had told myself I was going because of his birthday but that wasn't entirely true. I was going because I had been feeling like furniture. Present, functional, unremarkable. The kind of thing people look past while searching for something else in a room.

He made chicken. He always makes chicken when I come home, the same recipe he learned from my mother before she decided she'd rather live in Portugal than in a house where nobody talked about anything real. I don't blame her. I understood it even when I was twelve and didn't have the vocabulary for understanding it. Some people are suited for the long quiet and some people require light the way plants do, biologically, not as a preference.

We ate at the table he bought at an auction in 1987. There are rings on it from cups and the ghosts of homework assignments pressed too hard through thin paper. My initials are carved under the lip of the far left corner. I did it when I was nine and he caught me and I thought the world was ending and he just looked at the letters for a long moment and said, *now you'll always know which chair is yours.*

He asked me how work was going and I told him fine. He asked if I was eating enough and I said yes. He said the Hendersons' dog had gotten out again and torn through the garden and I nodded as if this was the kind of information that would stay with me, that I would carry it home and set it on my own counter next to my own broken things.

After dinner he turned on the television and I sat with him through an hour of a nature documentary about the Arctic. A polar bear moved across the ice with a patience that seemed almost spiritual. The narrator said something about how they can smell a seal beneath three feet of frozen water. We watched the bear lower its head and wait. We watched it wait for what felt like longer than television usually allows. Then the seal appeared and the bear moved and the narrator said *this is how they survive the season* and my father said, quietly, without looking away from the screen, *I'm glad you came.*

I didn't say anything. I'm not sure there was anything to say that wouldn't have been smaller than his words or wouldn't have broken the specific quality of the moment, which was rare and needed to be treated like something that could evaporate.

I slept in my old room under the quilt that still has a stain on the lower right corner from a bottle of nail polish I knocked over in high school. The ceiling had the same water mark shaped vaguely like Italy that I had memorized at seventeen while trying to understand things that weren't understandable yet. I lay there in the dark and listened to the house make its sounds. The way old houses do, the settling, the pipes, the particular creak of the third stair that has been creaking since before I was born, announcing nothing, announcing only: *here, here, here.*

In the morning I came downstairs and my father was already at the table with his coffee and the newspaper, though I know he reads everything on his phone now and the newspaper subscription is another monument to the same unnamed thing as the radio. He had made eggs. He was already eating his but mine were covered with a plate to keep them warm, sitting there waiting for me, a small act of faith that I would come downstairs, that I would be hungry, that there would be a morning and I would be in it.

I sat down. I moved the plate. The eggs were still warm.

We didn't talk much. We read things separately and commented occasionally on what we read the way people do when they've known each other long enough that conversation doesn't need architecture, it can just accumulate in the air like weather.

When I left he stood in the driveway in his flannel shirt and watched me back out. I watched him in the rearview mirror getting smaller the way people do. He raised one hand. Not a wave exactly. More like an acknowledgment. *I see you. I saw you.* I kept watching until I turned onto the county road and he was gone and then it was just flat country again, the grain elevators, the long gray sky.

The radio in my apartment works. I turn it on sometimes when I'm cooking, not because I need the noise but because silence can become a habit you feed without meaning to until it takes up all the space. I stand at the stove and listen to whatever comes through and think about the polar bear on the ice, the particular patience of it, the way it knew something was there beneath the surface even when the surface gave nothing away.

*How they survive the season.*

I stir the pot. The radio plays something I don't recognize. Outside it's starting to rain.
